messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

  "opinion": "As a fitness trainer in Kansas City who spends a few times a week on social media, I've noticed how fake content can derail my focus and even trigger my depression. My parents never had much education beyond basic schooling, so I've always been taught to question things before accepting them—like how I double-check the credibility of a workout video before sharing it with my clients. With my high conscientiousness and agreeableness, I tend to trust others' opinions, but I've learned that real health advice isn't just about what looks good on screen. I've started using simple filters on my phone to mute accounts that post misleading fitness tips, which has helped me stay grounded in reality. When I see fake news about health trends, I pause and think: 'What would my dad or mom say?' They'd probably tell me to check the source before reacting. My depression makes me more sensitive to online negativity, so I've cut back on social media during tough days and focus on my hobbies like football and cinema to reset my mind. The key isn't to avoid social media entirely but to be intentional about what I engage with—especially when my neuroticism might make me overreact. I've found that asking myself, 'Is this helpful for real life?' keeps me from getting caught in the cycle of misinformation.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As a fitness trainer in Kansas City who spends a few times a week on social media, I've noticed how fake content can derail my focus and even trigger my depression. My parents never had much education beyond basic schooling, so I've always been taught to question things before accepting them—like how I double-check the credibility of a workout video before sharing it with my clients. With my high conscientiousness and agreeableness, I tend to trust others' opinions, but I've learned that real health advice isn't just about what looks good on screen. I've started using simple filters on my phone to mute accounts that post misleading fitness tips, which has helped me stay grounded in reality. When I see fake news about health trends, I pause and think: 'What would my dad or mom say?' They'd probably tell me to check the source before reacting. My depression makes me more sensitive to online negativity, so I've cut back on social media during tough days and focus on my hobbies like football and cinema to reset my mind. The key isn't to avoid social media entirely but to be intentional about what I engage with—especially when my neuroticism might make me overreact. I've found that asking myself, 'Is this helpful for real life?' keeps me from getting caught in the cycle of misinformation.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 41-year-old married man with one child, who is depressed and has a high conscientiousness and agreeableness score. He spends a few times weekly on social media and has a background of limited formal education. His depression and high agreeableness make him susceptible to fake content, but his conscientiousness helps him implement practical strategies like using phone filters and questioning sources. The opinion focuses on his personal experience, leveraging his hobbies and family background to ground his approach. It avoids technical jargon, aligns with his modest education level, and addresses his mental health needs.
response_parsed.tone: Practical, reflective, grounded in personal experience with a touch of vulnerability
citations: Personal experience as a fitness trainer
